On 19 January 2026, its current name was adopted.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

**Anders** (Dutch: [[ˈɑndərs]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-anders.ogg "File:Nl-anders.ogg"), lit. ''Different''),[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) formerly known as the **Open Flemish Liberals and Democrats** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Open Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten*, [[ˈoːpə(ɱ) ˈvlaːmsə libəˈraːlən ɛn deːmoːˈkraːtə(n), -lə ʔɛn -]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Vlaamse_Liberalen_en_Democraten.ogg "File:Nl-Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten.ogg"), Open VLD),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a Flemish [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") political party in Belgium.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Politically liberal,[[7]](#cite_note-Slomp2011-7)[[8]](#cite_note-StarkeKaasch2013-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) the party has been described as [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right "Centre-right"),[[10]](#cite_note-Colomer2008-10) and has smaller factions within the party that have [conservative liberal](/wiki/Conservative_liberal "Conservative liberal"),[[7]](#cite_note-Slomp2011-7)[[8]](#cite_note-StarkeKaasch2013-8)[[11]](#cite_note-Spierings2019-11) as well as [social liberal](/wiki/Social_liberalism "Social liberalism") views.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The party is a member of the [Liberal Group](/wiki/Benelux_Parliament "Benelux Parliament"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Renew Europe](/wiki/Renew_Europe "Renew Europe"), and [Liberal International](/wiki/Liberal_International "Liberal International"). On 19 January 2026, its current name was adopted.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

The party was created in 1992 from the former bilingual [Party for Freedom and Progress](/wiki/Party_for_Freedom_and_Progress "Party for Freedom and Progress") (PVV) and politicians from other parties after Belgium was reconstituted as a federal state based on language, with the French-speaking faction forming the [Reformist Movement](/wiki/Reformist_Movement "Reformist Movement") (MR) in Wallonia. The party led the government for three cabinets under [Guy Verhofstadt](/wiki/Guy_Verhofstadt "Guy Verhofstadt") from 1999 until March 2008. Open VLD then formed the federal government (the "Swedish government") with the [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") (N-VA), [Christian Democratic and Flemish](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_and_Flemish "Christian Democratic and Flemish") (CD&V), and MR.

In the [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament"), the VLD formed a [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") with [sp.a-Spirit](/wiki/Sp.a-Spirit "Sp.a-Spirit") and CD&V from after the [2004 Belgian regional elections](/wiki/2004_Belgian_regional_elections "2004 Belgian regional elections") until the [2009 Belgian regional elections](/wiki/2009_Belgian_regional_elections "2009 Belgian regional elections"). Open Vld was a member of the [Leterme I Government](/wiki/Leterme_I_Government "Leterme I Government") formed on 22 March 2008, the [Van Rompuy I Government](/wiki/Van_Rompuy_I_Government "Van Rompuy I Government") formed on 2 January 2009, the [Leterme II Government](/wiki/Leterme_II_Government "Leterme II Government") formed on 24 November 2009, and the [Di Rupo Government](/wiki/Di_Rupo_Government "Di Rupo Government") formed on 6 December 2011.

Ideologically, Open VLD started as an [economically liberal](/wiki/Economically_liberal "Economically liberal"),[[13]](#cite_note-BanchoffSmith1999-13) in the mold of [Thatcherism](/wiki/Thatcherism "Thatcherism"), which mirrored some of the original ideology of the PVV. The VLD rapidly became more [centrist](/wiki/Centrist "Centrist") and gave up much of its [free-market](/wiki/Free-market "Free-market") approach, partly under the influence of Verhofstadt's political scientist brother [Dirk Verhofstadt](/wiki/Dirk_Verhofstadt "Dirk Verhofstadt"). The VLD continued to contain [conservative-libertarian](/wiki/Conservative-libertarian "Conservative-libertarian") and [classical liberal](/wiki/Classical_liberal "Classical liberal") wings with ties to think-tanks like [Nova Civitas](/wiki/Nova_Civitas "Nova Civitas"). Party chairman [Bart Somers](/wiki/Bart_Somers "Bart Somers") called in November 2006 for a "revolution" within the party, saying that "a liberal party", like the VLD, "can be only progressive and social".[[14]](#cite_note-14)

From 2000 to 2004, during the second period of its participation in the Belgian federal government and under [Belgian Prime Minister](/wiki/Belgian_Prime_Minister "Belgian Prime Minister") Verhofstadt, the VLD allegedly lost most of its ideological appeal. Several of its thinkers such as former member and [Nova Civitas](/wiki/Nova_Civitas "Nova Civitas") president [Boudewijn Bouckaert](/wiki/Boudewijn_Bouckaert "Boudewijn Bouckaert") heavily criticized the party. Many others, particularly from the party's conservative and Flemish autonomist wing, resented the priority it placed on the "Belgian compromise", which enabled the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(Belgium) "Socialist Party (Belgium)") (PS) of the [French Community of Belgium](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") to gain a dominant position in the formulation of Belgian federal government policy.

In 2004, the VLD teamed up with the minority social-liberal party [Vivant](/wiki/Vivant "Vivant") for both the Flemish and [European elections](/wiki/European_elections "European elections"). VLD-Vivant lost the elections to arch rivals CD&V and the [Flemish Bloc](/wiki/Flemish_Bloc "Flemish Bloc"). The VLD fell from second to third place among the Flemish political parties, slipping narrowly behind the sp.a-Spirit cartel. Internal feuds, the support for electoral rights for immigrants, and an unsuccessful economic policy were seen as the main reasons for its election defeat. From 2007, the party kept having electoral difficulties, first due to competition from split-off [List Dedecker](/wiki/List_Dedecker "List Dedecker") and after 2010 from the liberal-conservative [Flemish nationalist](/wiki/Flemish_nationalist "Flemish nationalist") party N-VA.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The VLD has its origins in the [Party for Freedom and Progress](/wiki/Party_for_Freedom_and_Progress "Party for Freedom and Progress") (which in turn was a successor to the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(Belgium) "Liberal Party (Belgium)")), a bilingual party which stood in both the Flemish and Walloon regions of Belgium. As such the liberal party is the oldest political party of Belgium. In 1846, [Walthère Frère-Orban](/wiki/Walth%C3%A8re_Fr%C3%A8re-Orban "Walthère Frère-Orban") succeeded in creating a political program which could unite several liberal groups into one party. Before 1960, the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(Belgium) "Liberal Party (Belgium)") of Belgium was barely organised. The school pact of 1958, as a result of which the most important argument for the traditional [anti-clericalism](/wiki/Anti-clericalism "Anti-clericalism") was removed, gave the necessary impetus for a thorough renewal. During the liberal party congress of 1961, the Liberal Party was reformed into the bilingual [Party for Freedom and Progress](/wiki/Party_for_Freedom_and_Progress "Party for Freedom and Progress") (PVV-PLP), and [Omer Vanaudenhove](/wiki/Omer_Vanaudenhove "Omer Vanaudenhove") became the chairman of the new party. The new liberal party, which struggled with an anti-clerical image, opened for believers, but was not too concerned about the situation of workers and primarily defended the interests of employers. It is a central principle of Classical Liberalism that employers and employees do not have opposed long term interests.

In the late 1960s and the early 1970s, the tensions between the different communities in Belgium rose and there were disagreements within the liberal movement as well. In 1972, the unitary PVV-PLP was split into separate a [Flemish](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and a [Francophone](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") parties. On Flemish side, under the guidance of [Frans Grootjans](/wiki/Frans_Grootjans "Frans Grootjans"), [Herman Vanderpoorten](/wiki/Herman_Vanderpoorten "Herman Vanderpoorten") and [Willy De Clercq](/wiki/Willy_De_Clercq "Willy De Clercq"), the PVV was created, on Walloon side [Milou Jeunehomme](/wiki/Milou_Jeunehomme "Milou Jeunehomme") became the head of the PLP and Brussels got its own but totally disintegrated liberal party landscape. Willy De Clercq became the first chairman of the independent Party of Freedom and Progress ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Partij voor Vrijheid en Vooruitgang*, PVV). De Clercq, together with Frans Grootjans and Herman Vanderpoorten, set out the lines for the new party. This reform was coupled an Ethical Congress, on which the PVV adopted progressive and tolerant stances regarding abortion, [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia "Euthanasia"), [adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery"), homosexuality, and [gender equality](/wiki/Gender_equality "Gender equality").

In 1982, the 29-year-old reformer [Guy Verhofstadt](/wiki/Guy_Verhofstadt "Guy Verhofstadt") became the chairman of the party, and even was Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Budget from 1986 to 1988. [Annemie Neyts](/wiki/Annemie_Neyts "Annemie Neyts") succeeded him as chairman, becoming the first female party chairman. In 1989, Verhofstadt once more became the chairman of the PVV, after his party had been condemned to the opposition by the [Christian People's Party](/wiki/Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams "Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams") (CVP) in 1987. In 1992, the PVV was reformed into the Flemish Liberals and Democrats (*Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten*, VLD) under the impulse of Verhofstadt. Although the VLD was the successor of the PVV, many politicians with democratic nationalist or socialist roots joined the new party. Notable examples are [Jaak Gabriëls](/wiki/Jaak_Gabri%C3%ABls "Jaak Gabriëls"), then-president of the Flemish [People's Union](/wiki/People%27s_Union_(Belgium) "People's Union (Belgium)"), and [Hugo Coveliers](/wiki/Hugo_Coveliers "Hugo Coveliers"). From the early 1990s, the VLD advanced in every election, only to get in government following the [1999 Belgian federal election](/wiki/1999_Belgian_federal_election "1999 Belgian federal election") when the VLD became the largest party. Verhofstadt became [Prime Minister of Belgium](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Belgium "Prime Minister of Belgium") and [Patrick Dewael](/wiki/Patrick_Dewael "Patrick Dewael") became [Minister-President of Flanders](/wiki/List_of_Minister-Presidents_of_Flanders "List of Minister-Presidents of Flanders"). They were both at the head of a coalition of liberals, social democrats, and [greens](/wiki/Green_politics "Green politics").

### 2007 elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2007 elections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Open_VLD.svg/250px-Open_VLD.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Open_VLD.svg)

The logo for the party when it was known as Open VLD.

Main article: [2007 Belgian federal election](/wiki/2007_Belgian_federal_election "2007 Belgian federal election")

Before the 2007 general election, the VLD participated in a cartel with [Vivant](/wiki/Vivant "Vivant") and [Liberal Appeal](/wiki/Liberal_Appeal "Liberal Appeal"). In February 2007, it decided to cease the cartel and start operating under the name **Open VLD**. On 10 June 2007, Open VLD won 18 out of 150 seats in the [Chamber of Representatives](/wiki/Belgian_Chamber_of_Representatives "Belgian Chamber of Representatives") and five out of 40 seats in the [Senate](/wiki/Belgian_Senate "Belgian Senate").

### 2010 elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2010 elections")]

Main articles: [2010 Belgian federal election](/wiki/2010_Belgian_federal_election "2010 Belgian federal election") and [2010–2011 Belgian government formation](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_Belgian_government_formation "2010–2011 Belgian government formation")

In the 2010 general election, Open VLD won 13 out of 150 seats in the [Chamber of Representatives](/wiki/Belgian_Chamber_of_Representatives "Belgian Chamber of Representatives"). After the long [government formation process](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_Belgian_government_formation "2010–2011 Belgian government formation"), on 6 December 2011 the [Di Rupo Government](/wiki/Di_Rupo_Government "Di Rupo Government") was formed, with Open VLD one of the six constituent parties.

### 2014 elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2014 elections")]

Main article: [2014 Belgian federal election](/wiki/2014_Belgian_federal_election "2014 Belgian federal election")

The party saw a modest increase in support in the 2014 general election, winning 14 out of 150 seats in the Chamber of Representatives. Following the elections, the party was part of a five-party coalition ([Michel I Government](/wiki/Michel_I_Government "Michel I Government")) that saw [Charles Michel](/wiki/Charles_Michel "Charles Michel") become Prime Minister.

### 2019 elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2019 elections")]

Main articles: [2019 Belgian federal election](/wiki/2019_Belgian_federal_election "2019 Belgian federal election") and [2019–2020 Belgian government formation](/wiki/2019%E2%80%932020_Belgian_government_formation "2019–2020 Belgian government formation")

The 2019 general election saw a small decrease in support for Open VLD, with the party winning 12 out of 150 seats in the Chamber of Representatives. This election led to a prolonged government negotiation, which ultimately resulted in Open VLD member [Alexander De Croo](/wiki/Alexander_De_Croo "Alexander De Croo") becoming Prime Minister over a year after the election took place. The [De Croo Government](/wiki/De_Croo_Government "De Croo Government") was a seven-party coalition with support coming from the moderate right, social democrats, and greens in both the Flemish and Francophone communities.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### 2024 elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2024 elections")]

Main article: [2024 Belgian federal election](/wiki/2024_Belgian_federal_election "2024 Belgian federal election")

The 2024 general election saw the worst result in history for Open VLD, being reduced to eight seats in the Chamber of Representatives. De Croo resigned as Prime Minister in the aftermath, and in 2025 the [De Wever government](/wiki/De_Wever_government "De Wever government") was formed excluding Open VLD for the first time since the [1995 Belgian federal election](/wiki/1995_Belgian_federal_election "1995 Belgian federal election").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The party adopted its current name in January 2026, dropping the *Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten* for the first time since its founding.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

At its inception, the Open VLD was a [classical liberal](/wiki/Classical_liberal "Classical liberal") and somewhat [right-libertarian](/wiki/Right-libertarian "Right-libertarian") party with support for free-markets and deregulation. Former party leader [Guy Verhofstadt](/wiki/Guy_Verhofstadt "Guy Verhofstadt") was compared to [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") in his beliefs during his time as party chairman. In the 1990s, the party switched from a right-libertarian to a more [socially liberal](/wiki/Socially_liberal "Socially liberal") position under [Bart Somers](/wiki/Bart_Somers "Bart Somers"). Some of the party's ideological influences have been [Karl Popper](/wiki/Karl_Popper "Karl Popper"), [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill"), [Thomas Paine](/wiki/Thomas_Paine "Thomas Paine"), [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen"), and [Martha Nussbaum](/wiki/Martha_Nussbaum "Martha Nussbaum"). The party also contained members from both [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") and [Flemish nationalists](/wiki/Flemish_nationalists "Flemish nationalists") with [liberal-conservative](/wiki/Liberal-conservative "Liberal-conservative") backgrounds who influenced the VLD's course, such as former [Volksunie](/wiki/Volksunie "Volksunie") leader [Jaak Gabriëls](/wiki/Jaak_Gabri%C3%ABls "Jaak Gabriëls") and [Hugo Coveliers](/wiki/Hugo_Coveliers "Hugo Coveliers") falling into the latter camp. Others had ties to the conservative-libertarian organization [Nova Civitas](/wiki/Nova_Civitas "Nova Civitas"), and were open to working with the [Vlaams Blok](/wiki/Vlaams_Blok "Vlaams Blok") and later [Vlaams Belang](/wiki/Vlaams_Belang "Vlaams Belang") party, although this was strongly opposed by the party leadership as a whole.

The Open VLD retains an economically liberal position by supporting lower taxes and private property ownership while also closing tax loopholes. It supports a Canadian model of migration and for a more inclusive society towards immigrants, but claims not to endorse open borders and wants quicker deportation of illegal immigrants. It also retains a socially liberal stance on matters such as same-sex marriage, LGBT rights, and introducing a [third gender](/wiki/Third_gender "Third gender") option on official documents, but also believes the government should not interfere with matters related to sexuality.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

For a period, the party was considered the main centre-right rival to the Christian Democratic [CD&V](/wiki/CD%26V "CD&V") in the Flemish region and saw its highest period of support in the late 1990s and early 2000s. However, the emergence of the Flemish nationalist [N-VA](/wiki/N-VA "N-VA") party and the [LDD](/wiki/Libertarian,_Direct,_Democratic "Libertarian, Direct, Democratic") drew some of the party's conservative leaning voters away and contributed to a decline in votes. Ideological disputes also caused some of the party's more conservative and traditionalist libertarian wing such as [Boudewijn Bouckaert](/wiki/Boudewijn_Bouckaert "Boudewijn Bouckaert"), [Jean-Marie Dedecker](/wiki/Jean-Marie_Dedecker "Jean-Marie Dedecker") and [Hugo Coveliers](/wiki/Hugo_Coveliers "Hugo Coveliers") to leave the party. Dedecker later founded the LDD and Coveliers [VLOTT](/wiki/VLOTT "VLOTT") while others joined the N-VA.[[13]](#cite_note-BanchoffSmith1999-13) In 2024, Open VLD (along with [Reformist Movement](/wiki/Reformist_Movement "Reformist Movement")) blocked Belgium from [recognizing](/wiki/International_recognition_of_the_State_of_Palestine "International recognition of the State of Palestine") the [State of Palestine](/wiki/State_of_Palestine "State of Palestine").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Representation in EU institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Representation in EU institutions")]

The party is fairly [pro-European](/wiki/Pro-Europeanism "Pro-Europeanism"),[[19]](#cite_note-Almeida107-19) and sits in the [Renew Europe](/wiki/Renew_Europe "Renew Europe") group with two MEPs.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Then-Prime Minister Verhofstadt (VLD) was rejected as a candidate for the presidency of the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") in June 2004. In the [European Committee of the Regions](/wiki/European_Committee_of_the_Regions "European Committee of the Regions"), Open VLD sits in the [Renew Europe CoR group](/wiki/Renew_Europe_in_the_European_Committee_of_the_Regions "Renew Europe in the European Committee of the Regions"), with one alternate members for the 2025-2030 mandate.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Jean-Luc Vanraes is Coordinator in the CIVEX Commission.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Notable former members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anders_(political_party)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Notable former members")]

* [Boudewijn Bouckaert](/wiki/Boudewijn_Bouckaert "Boudewijn Bouckaert"), a former VLD board member who left the party subsequently to Dedecker's exclusion, believing the party turned "left-liberal". He and Dedecker are founders of a new political party, List Dedecker, later renamed [Libertarian, Direct, Democratic](/wiki/Libertair,_Direct,_Democratisch "Libertair, Direct, Democratisch").
* [Hugo Coveliers](/wiki/Hugo_Coveliers "Hugo Coveliers"), left the VLD to found his own political party [VLOTT](/wiki/VLOTT "VLOTT").
* [Jean-Marie Dedecker](/wiki/Jean-Marie_Dedecker "Jean-Marie Dedecker"), was excluded from the VLD after several conflicts with the top of the party. He asked for an economic policy more in favour of free markets and limited government and believed that the party was too closely aligned with the Socialists. He founded the List Dedecker party, later [Libertarian, Direct, Democratic](/wiki/Libertair,_Direct,_Democratisch "Libertair, Direct, Democratisch").
* [Leo Govaerts](/w/index.php?title=Leo_Govaerts&action=edit&redlink=1 "Leo Govaerts (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leo_Govaerts "nl:Leo Govaerts")], left the VLD to found his own political party Veilig Blauw (*Safe Blue*).
* [Ward Beysen](/wiki/Ward_Beysen "Ward Beysen"), left the VLD to found his own political party [Liberal Appeal](/wiki/Liberal_Appeal "Liberal Appeal").
* [Sihame El Kaouakibi](/wiki/Sihame_El_Kaouakibi "Sihame El Kaouakibi"), left the VLD after claims of embezzlement.
